questions.text: The property of a body to regain its original shape after removal of deforming force is:
questions.explanation: Elasticity is the restoring property of materials.

questions.text: Stress is defined as:
options: Change in length per unit length
questions.explanation: Internal restoring force generated per unit area is stress.

questions.text: The SI unit of stress is same as that of:
questions.explanation: Both Stress and Pressure are N/m² (Pascal).

questions.text: Hooke's Law is valid within:
questions.explanation: Stress is proportional to strain only within the elastic limit.

questions.text: The ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain is:
questions.explanation: Young's Modulus (Y) applies to stretching or compression of wires/rods.

questions.text: Which of the following materials is more elastic?
questions.explanation: In physics, elasticity is measured by the modulus. Steel requires more force for the same strain than rubber.

questions.text: The ratio of change in volume to original volume is:
questions.explanation: ΔV/V is volume strain.

questions.text: Bulk Modulus is defined as:
questions.explanation: B = -ΔP / (ΔV/V).

questions.text: Modulus of Rigidity is associated with change in:
questions.explanation: Shear modulus (Rigidity) involves the displacement of layers, changing shape but not volume.

questions.text: Poisson's ratio is the ratio of:
questions.explanation: It describes how a material thins when stretched.

questions.text: Elastic potential energy stored per unit volume in a stretched wire is:
questions.explanation: Energy density = ½ × Stress × Strain.

questions.text: The theoretical limit of Poisson's ratio is between:
questions.explanation: Practically it is usually between 0 and 0.5 for most solids.

questions.text: A substance that can be elastically stretched to large strains is called:
questions.explanation: Examples include rubber and the tissue of the aorta.

questions.text: Breaking stress depends on:
questions.explanation: It is a characteristic property of the material.

questions.text: Compressibility is the reciprocal of:
If the temperature increases, the Young's modulus of a material:
questions.explanation: Increasing temperature increases intermolecular distance, making the material less stiff.

questions.text: The point on stress-strain curve where strain increases without further increase in stress is:
questions.explanation: Also called the flow point.

questions.text: Materials which break immediately after the elastic limit is crossed are:
questions.explanation: Example: Glass or Cast Iron.

questions.text: Units of Strain are:
questions.explanation: Strain is a ratio of similar quantities (e.g., L/L).

questions.text: Ductile materials have a _____ region of plastic deformation.
questions.explanation: This allows them to be drawn into thin wires.

questions.text: The slope of Stress vs Strain graph in the elastic region gives:
questions.explanation: From Hooke's Law, Stress = E × Strain.

questions.text: Shear Modulus is also known as:
questions.explanation: It relates to the stiffness of a solid against shape change.

questions.text: In a wire stretched by a weight, the work done is stored as:
questions.explanation: U = ½ F ΔL.

questions.text: Which of the following has NO modulus of elasticity?
questions.explanation: Liquids and gases only have Bulk Modulus; they cannot sustain longitudinal or shear stress.

questions.text: Hydraulic stress is always _____ to the surface.
questions.explanation: It acts equally from all directions perpendicular to the surface.

questions.text: For an ideal rigid body, Young's modulus is:
questions.explanation: A rigid body does not deform at all, implying infinite stiffness.

questions.text: The length of a wire is doubled. Its Young's modulus will:
questions.explanation: Young's modulus is a material property, not dependent on dimensions.

questions.text: Strain is a _____ quantity.
questions.explanation: It has no units or dimensions.

questions.text: Metals are usually _____ than elastomers.
questions.explanation: In technical terms, a higher modulus means higher elasticity.

questions.text: The area under Stress-Strain curve up to the elastic limit represents:
questions.explanation: Integrated stress over strain gives energy per unit volume.

questions.text: Spring balance works on the principle of:
questions.explanation: Extension is proportional to load.

questions.text: Ratio of stress to strain is constant. This is:
questions.explanation: Basic law of elasticity.

questions.text: Which state of matter has the highest Bulk Modulus?
questions.explanation: Solids are hardest to compress.

questions.text: Elastic fatigue is:
options: Loss of strength due to repeated stress
questions.explanation: Material becomes weak after many cycles.

questions.text: Young's modulus is for:
questions.explanation: Only solids sustain length change.

questions.text: Shear strain is measured by:
questions.explanation: Usually denoted by θ or φ.

questions.text: An incompressible liquid has Bulk Modulus:
questions.explanation: ΔV=0 implies B is infinity.

questions.text: Unit of Modulus of Elasticity is:
questions.explanation: Stress/Strain -> N/m².

questions.text: Elastic after-effect is:
questions.explanation: Observed in glass and silk.

questions.text: Gases have Bulk Modulus of the order of:
questions.explanation: Atmospheric pressure level.
